title: Simulating everything, sort of: The promise and limits of world models
gist: A deep-dive interview piece with researchers from MIT, Runway, and World Labs shows 'world models' emerging as a well-funded ($1B+ rounds for World Labs and Yann LeCun's AMI, $315M for Runway) alternative/complement to LLMs aimed at robotics, 3D asset generation, and physical simulation, but the field is fragmented across incompatible architectures (video-diffusion vs. explicit-3D vs. JEPA-style latent prediction) with no consensus on interfaces, and even its practitioners admit reliability as general-purpose physical simulators remains unproven.

themes.theme: Capital flows and the post-LLM narrative

insights.claim: World Labs and Yann LeCun's new company Advanced Machine Intelligence (AMI) each reportedly raised around $1 billion in February and March respectively, and Runway raised $315 million in February, marking a rapid escalation of world-model funding within a matter of months.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: Roughly $2.3B+ deployed into world-model startups in a single quarter signals a distinct, well-capitalized investment category diverging from pure-play LLM startups; investors should track World Labs, AMI, and Runway as the leading pure-play names in this emerging category alongside Google DeepMind's Genie 3.

insights.claim: Hugging Face CEO Clem Delangue said he believes the LLM bubble might burst next year, arguing that LLMs are only a subset of AI applicable to biology, chemistry, image, audio, and video, and that much more will be seen in adjacent AI categories in coming years.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: A prominent AI infrastructure CEO publicly flagging LLM-bubble risk is a signal for investors to diversify AI exposure beyond pure LLM plays toward multimodal/world-model and non-language AI categories.
insights.quote: I think we’re in an LLM bubble, and I think the LLM bubble might be bursting next year

insights.claim: Former Meta chief AI scientist Yann LeCun argues that extending LLM capabilities will never reach human-level intelligence, and has founded AMI specifically betting that AI systems which interact with or simulate the physical world, not language models, represent the real path forward.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: One of the most credentialed AI researchers has left Meta to bet roughly $1B of investor capital against the dominant LLM-scaling paradigm; a contrarian, well-funded position worth tracking as a hedge/alternative thesis to continued LLM capex dominance.
insights.quote: The idea that you’re going to extend the capabilities of LLMs to the point that they’re going to have human-level intelligence is complete nonsense

insights.claim: World Labs co-founder Fei-Fei Li argues that LLMs remain 'wordsmiths in the dark' — eloquent but ungrounded — and that 'spatial intelligence,' the domain of world models, represents AI's next frontier for storytelling, robotics, and scientific discovery.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: A leading computer-vision researcher explicitly frames spatial/world models as the successor investment frontier to LLMs, reinforcing the thesis that capital and talent are rotating toward this category.
insights.quote: Today, leading AI technology such as large language models (LLMs) have begun to transform how we access and work with abstract knowledge. Yet they remain wordsmiths in the dark; eloquent but inexperienced, knowledgeable but ungrounded.

themes.theme: What a 'world model' actually is — definitional fragmentation

insights.claim: MIT's Vincent Sitzmann defines a world model broadly as any model that takes in an interaction and, given that interaction, enables simulation of what would happen next in an environment — while acknowledging the term is 'definitely an overloaded term' used differently by every company.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: Definitional ambiguity across 'world model' branding (Runway's GWM-1, World Labs' Marble, Google DeepMind's Genie 3, LeCun's JEPA) means investors must evaluate each company's specific technical approach rather than treat 'world model' as a single interchangeable investment category.

insights.claim: World Labs co-founder Ben Mildenhall distinguishes world models from LLMs by their real-time, continuous, synchronous interaction model versus the LLM's turn-based text-in/text-out pattern, framing world models as systems users interact with as if inside a persistent, parallel-updating world.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: This framing implies world models could require fundamentally different (and likely more compute-intensive, latency-sensitive) infrastructure than LLM serving stacks, a factor relevant to cloud/inference infrastructure providers sizing future compute demand.
insights.quote: The key things that would distinguish it from an LLM are demonstrating degrees of spatial and—maybe for lack of a better word—continuous understanding

insights.claim: The term 'world model' is becoming as much a marketing/branding label as a technical one, covering everything from action-conditioned video generation to 3D asset creation to robot policy evaluation, with different companies solving different technical problems under the same umbrella term.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: Investors should discount 'world model' branding in pitch decks and press releases and instead diligence the underlying technical approach (video diffusion, explicit 3D, or JEPA-style latent prediction) before assuming comparability between companies.

themes.theme: Competing technical architectures and their tradeoffs

insights.claim: Runway's GWM-1 uses autoregressive diffusion — denoising one or a few video frames at a time so users can inject actions that influence subsequent frames — instead of the standard video-diffusion approach of denoising all frames simultaneously, trading full-video coherence for real-time interactivity.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: Autoregressive diffusion is extremely compute-expensive because each batch of frames requires a full denoising pass in near real time, implying world-model interactivity at scale will demand significant new inference compute investment, a potential tailwind for GPU/inference infrastructure providers.
insights.quote: So the difference with the autoregressive diffusion, which is the approach that we’re taking with GWM, is you’re essentially denoising one or a few frames at a time.

insights.claim: Runway's world models still suffer from context drift — the simulation can forget details of an earlier environment (e.g., a room a user previously exited and returned to) — an unresolved technical limitation even in the most cutting-edge frontier world models as of this reporting.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: Persistent-memory/statefulness is an open technical gap across the leading commercial world models, a concrete engineering risk factor to track before assuming near-term production-readiness for long-duration robotics or simulation applications.
insights.quote: If you’re in a room, you exit the room, you go back to the room, you need to make sure that all the details of the room are preserved inside the context and the memory of the model

insights.claim: World Labs' current product Marble outputs static 3D scenes (via NeRFs and Gaussian splats) that can be freely navigated and rendered on consumer 3D hardware without further neural network compute once generated, but has no dynamics — nothing in the generated scene moves or reacts to physics — a tradeoff against Runway's dynamic but far more compute-expensive frame-based approach.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: World Labs' bake-once, render-cheap approach is commercially practical today (usable by game/VFX studios immediately) but structurally limited for robotics/dynamic simulation use cases, while Runway's approach is more capable but not yet economically viable at scale — investors should map each company's roadmap against this fundamental static-vs-dynamic tradeoff rather than assume convergence is imminent.
insights.quote: The key benefit of having a 3D scene is that you need to generate it only once

insights.claim: Yann LeCun's JEPA architecture, used at AMI, takes a different approach entirely by predicting the abstract state of a scene rather than generating every future pixel, aiming to learn only the structure relevant for prediction and planning without the massive compute cost of modeling irrelevant visual detail.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: JEPA represents a potentially far more compute-efficient alternative to video-diffusion-based world models; if it proves viable, it could undercut the massive inference-compute cost structure implied by Runway's and others' pixel-generation approaches — a key technical bet to monitor for capital efficiency in this category.

insights.claim: World Labs' Mildenhall said the underlying technical backbone (transformers, diffusion) is largely shared across most world-model approaches, and the real unresolved question is the input-output structure and control interface, not the foundational architecture itself.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: Suggests less technical moat/differentiation at the architecture level between competitors than product/interface-level differentiation, implying competitive advantage in this space will come from product design and data/use-case specialization rather than proprietary model architecture alone.
insights.quote: It’s not like a fork at the root. It’s really like a branch in the application side.

themes.theme: The 'bitter lesson' and the bet against hand-engineered 3D/physics priors

insights.claim: Runway CTO Anastasis Germanidis said the company deliberately avoids injecting explicit 3D-scene representations into its models, betting instead that 3D consistency and statefulness will emerge purely from scale and pixel-prediction training, following reinforcement-learning pioneer Richard Sutton's 'bitter lesson' that hand-engineered human priors are historically outperformed by scaled compute and learning.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: This is the same scaling bet that powered LLM progress being re-applied to world models; if it holds, it favors well-capitalized players who can out-scale compute (Runway, World Labs, AMI, Google DeepMind) over specialized engineering-heavy simulation companies, reinforcing compute/capital intensity as the core competitive lever in this category.
insights.quote: Our overall approach is that 3D consistency and that statefulness should emerge from scale and from just predicting 2D pixels

insights.claim: MIT's Sitzmann says the field's core, still-undecided bet is whether relying on latent (learned, non-human-readable) representations of 3D scenes and physics — rather than explicit human-engineered 3D/physics priors — is the right path, explicitly calling it unresolved: 'I'm saying it's a bet. This is not a decided thing.'
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: A leading academic researcher's explicit acknowledgment of high uncertainty on the field's central architectural bet should temper investor confidence in any single world-model approach being a clear technology winner in the near term.
insights.quote: I’m saying it’s a bet. This is not a decided thing

themes.theme: Robotics: the real near-term commercial application and its data bottleneck

insights.claim: Robotics progress is bottlenecked by a lack of paired perception-action training data at scale, unlike self-driving cars where abundant dashcam and human-driving data exists; humanoid robots operating in homes or workplaces face far more varied situations with comparatively little useful training data, and companies paying people to wear cameras during chores are unlikely to produce sufficient data on their own.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: Identifies the core commercial rationale for world models in robotics: using video world models to generate synthetic perception-action training data as a workaround to the real-world data scarcity problem, positioning world-model companies as potential infrastructure suppliers to humanoid robotics firms (e.g., Figure, Tesla Optimus, Boston Dynamics) rather than direct competitors.
insights.quote: The core challenge of embodied intelligence is the lack of paired perception–action data at scale.

insights.claim: Runway's Germanidis said video world models could generate large volumes of synthetic training data of robots performing tasks that are hard, slow, or unsafe to collect via real-world teleoperation, positioning world models as a potential data-generation bottleneck-breaker for robotics.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: If validated, this would materially lower the cost and time-to-scale for humanoid/robotics companies training complex manipulation policies, a potential accelerant for robotics investment broadly if world-model-generated synthetic data proves reliable.
insights.quote: You might want to use a video model to generate a lot of synthetic data of the robot performing different kinds of tasks

insights.claim: The reliability of world models as general-purpose physical simulators for robotics remains unproven; synthetic training data could teach wrong lessons if it misrepresents contact forces or surface friction, and small mismatches between simulation and reality can break robot policies, with the generalization gap not yet solved.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: A direct caution against over-extrapolating from polished robotics demo videos (which the article notes are often carefully curated); investors evaluating robotics or world-model companies should demand evidence of policy performance on held-out, non-tuned tasks with failure cases included, not curated demos.

insights.claim: Impressive robotics demo videos showing humanoid or other robots performing previously-thought-impossible tasks can be misleading because environments, tasks, and objects are often carefully selected to showcase best-case capability that has not been shown to generalize.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: A direct warning against taking robotics company demo reels at face value when evaluating investment claims; the real test is performance on held-out tasks and environments not tuned for the demo.
insights.locator: This is one of many reasons why impressive marketing videos

insights.claim: Unlike LLMs, which have a settled, intuitive chat interface, world models have no settled human interface — possibilities range from game-controller/avatar navigation to Blender/Unreal-style scene views to interfaces meant for robots or other AI systems rather than humans at all — with World Labs' Mildenhall calling this representation question the real 'kicker' for the field.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: Unlike the LLM wave, where ChatGPT's chat interface became a near-universal product pattern quickly, world models face a much longer and more fragmented path to product-market fit since the interface itself is unresolved — a reason to expect a slower, more segmented commercialization timeline than LLMs had.

insights.claim: Runway chose to launch three separate specialized models under its GWM-1 banner (human interaction/navigation, character generation, and robot policy execution/evaluation) for speed-to-market and easier customer education, even though CTO Germanidis said the company ultimately believes in one unified model and plans to merge them later.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: Reveals a near-term product strategy tension between commercial pragmatism (specialized models shipping faster) and the long-term technical bet on unified/generalized models, useful for assessing Runway's roadmap and whether near-term specialized products will be superseded by a future unified model.
insights.quote: We’re big believers in having one big model that solves a lot of tasks versus having a lot of specialized models

insights.claim: World Labs' Mildenhall predicts AI-driven world-model tooling could reduce longstanding fragmentation in 3D asset formats across industries — for example, letting film studios like Disney more easily adapt movie-quality 3D assets for use in video games, a problem Epic Games (maker of Unreal Engine) is already separately working to address.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: If realized, this would lower production costs for cross-media content pipelines (film-to-game asset reuse), a potential efficiency tailwind for entertainment/gaming companies and a use case to watch for World Labs' commercial traction beyond standalone scene generation.
